claim: Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B achieves the highest overall average on AgentWorldBench (58.71) among all evaluated models, surpassing GPT-5.4 (58.25) and Claude Opus 4.6 (57.80).
Qwen-AgentWorld covers seven agent interaction domains within a single model: MCP, Search, Terminal, SWE, Android, Web, and OS.
Training uses a three-stage pipeline: CPT injects world knowledge, SFT activates next-state-prediction reasoning, and RL sharpens simulation fidelity with hybrid rubric-and-rule rewards.
AgentWorldBench contains 2,170 turn-level evaluation samples across 7 domains, built from real environment interactions of 5 frontier models on 9 established benchmarks.
Training Stage 3 (RL) on Terminal data alone improves Terminal by +14.2 points and transfers to held-out domains: MCP (+5.0), SWE (+11.5), and Search (+11.8).
Sim RL with Qwen-AgentWorld as simulator on 4k synthesized OpenClaw environments improves Claw-Eval by +4.3 and QwenClawBench by +7.1.
Controllable Sim RL on WideSearch using fictional-world simulation improves F1 by Item from 34.02 to 50.31 (+16.29) at 35B scale.
LWM RL warm-up on single-turn, non-agentic trajectories transfers to multi-turn, tool-calling agentic tasks across 7 benchmarks without additional fine-tuning.
Prediction accuracy on Terminal-Bench 2.0 trajectories improves from 69.9% to 78.3% (+8.4%) after LWM RL.
The hybrid reward combines a five-dimensional LLM judge rubric and rule-based verifiers at a 9:1 ratio (rubric:rule).
Information-theoretic loss masking uses four statistics (Overlap, Novelty, Jaccard, length ratio) to categorize turns into seven semantic categories with different keep ratios.
SFT uses 7,094 trajectories after rejection sampling from 10,250 candidates (69.2% retention rate).
Cross-judge Spearman rank correlations for model rankings are rho = 0.92-0.99 (all p < 10^-5) across Gemini 3 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and GPT-5.2.
Search is the most challenging domain for all models, with the best score (37.82) roughly half the best score on SWE (68.49).
The model exhibits 1,347 'Wait!' self-correction interrupts across 129 thinking traces (10.4 per turn on average).
Any agent capable of generalizing across a sufficiently broad range of tasks must have learned a world model (Richens et al., 2025).
source: Cited theoretical result from Richens et al., 2025

claim: GUI domain gaps reflect an advantage from multimodal pre-training that text-only world modeling does not fully capture.
Fictional-world Sim RL generalizes effectively to real-world search tasks because capabilities like query reformulation and multi-source cross-referencing transfer structurally.
